OpenClaw.title: OpenClaw
OpenClaw.content:
OpenClaw
OpenClaw — AI assistant that runs locally and connects to various messaging platforms.
Commands





































CommandDescription/newStart a fresh session/resetReset current session/statusShow session status, model, usage/reasoningToggle reasoning mode on/off/helpShow all available commands/model <name>Switch model for current session/thinkEnable verbose thinking
Gateway Management
openclaw gateway status      # Check if gateway is running
openclaw gateway start       # Start the gateway service
openclaw gateway stop        # Stop the gateway service
openclaw gateway restart     # Restart the gateway
Common Workflows
Daily Check-in

Check emails: /email or check my inbox
Check calendar: /calendar or what's on my schedule
Get weather: how's the weather today

Obsidian Workflow

Create note: Use QuickAdd with Cmd+N
Search notes: find note about <topic>
Update daily note: add X to today's reflection

Code Tasks

Delegate to subagent: /code <task description>
Review PR: /review <pr-url>

Tips

Voice messages: Send voice notes and I’ll transcribe/respond
Images: Send screenshots or photos directly
Context: I remember our conversation within a session
Memory: I write notes to memory/ folder for continuity between sessions

Running Modes
Prevent macOS Sleep (Caffeinate)
Keep your Mac awake while OpenClaw runs:
# Prevent sleep for 8 hours
caffeinate -u -t 28800 &
 
# Keep awake indefinitely (until logout/reboot)
caffeinate -i -s &
Or use the Caffeine app (from App Store or Homebrew):
brew install --cask caffeine
Headless Mode (Gateway Background)
Run the gateway as a background service:
# Start as background service
nohup caffeinate -is gateway start > openclaw.log 2>&1 &
 
# Check status
openclaw gateway status
 
# Run in foreground with verbose logging
openclaw gateway run --verbose
 
# Run gateway on specific port
openclaw gateway run --port 8080
Stop/Restart Gateway
openclaw gateway stop          # Stop the service
openclaw gateway restart       # Restart the service
openclaw gateway uninstall    # Remove service (rarely needed)
Resources

Docs: /Users/gsong/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/docs
Skills: ~/.openclaw/skills/
Workspace: ~/.openclaw/workspace/

Plugin Troubleshooting
memos-local Plugin Fix
# 1. Navigate to the plugin directory
cd /Users/gsong/.openclaw/extensions/memos-local-openclaw-plugin
 
# 2. Manually provide Node v25.2.1 headers to bypass the SSL download error
mkdir -p ~/.node-gyp/25.2.1
curl -kL nodejs.org/download/release/v25.2.1/node-v25.2.1-headers.tar.gz -o /tmp/node-headers.tar.gz
tar -xf /tmp/node-headers.tar.gz -C ~/.node-gyp/25.2.1 --strip-components=1
touch ~/.node-gyp/25.2.1/install.lock
 
# 3. Clean the previous failed installations
rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json
 
# 4. Set environment flags to skip failing downloads and ignore SSL certificate issues
export PUPPETEER_SKIP_DOWNLOAD=true
export NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0
 
# 5. Force install the latest compatible versions of native modules
npm install better-sqlite3@latest sharp@latest node-addon-api --save --strict-ssl=false
 
# 6. Run the final install for remaining dependencies
npm install --strict-ssl=false
 
# 7. Restart the OpenClaw gateway in headless mode
pkill -f openclaw
nohup caffeinate -is gateway start > openclaw.log 2>&1 &
 
# 8. Verify the status
sleep 2
openclaw status

Channel Setup
Discord
Setup Steps
Step 0: Create a Discord server
Step 1: Create a Discord application and bot

Go to the Discord Developer Portal and click New Application. Name it “OpenClaw”.
Click Bot on the sidebar. Set the Username to whatever you call your OpenClaw agent.

Step 2: Enable privileged intents
Still on the Bot page, scroll down to Privileged Gateway Intents and enable:

Message Content Intent (required)
Server Members Intent (recommended; required for role allowlists and name-to-ID matching)
Presence Intent (optional)

Step 3: Copy your bot token

Scroll back up on the Bot page and click Reset Token (this generates your first token).
Copy and save the token — you’ll need it shortly.

Step 4: Generate an invite URL and add the bot to your server

Click OAuth2 on the sidebar.
Scroll down to OAuth2 URL Generator and enable: bot and applications.commands.
In Bot Permissions, enable: View Channels, Send Messages, Read Message History, Embed Links, Attach Files.
Copy the generated URL, paste into your browser, select your server, and click Continue.

Step 5: Enable Developer Mode and collect IDs
In the Discord app:

Click User Settings → Advanced → toggle on Developer Mode
Right-click your server icon → Copy Server ID
Right-click your own avatar → Copy User ID

Step 6: Allow DMs from server members
Right-click your server icon → Privacy Settings → toggle on Direct Messages.
Step 7: Set bot token securely
openclaw config set channels.discord.token '"YOUR_BOT_TOKEN"' --json
openclaw config set channels.discord.enabled true --json
openclaw gateway restart
Step 8: Configure OpenClaw and pair
Tell your agent (via Telegram or another channel):

“I already set my Discord bot token in config. Please finish Discord setup with User ID <user_id> and Server ID <server_id>.”

Step 9: Approve pairing

DM your bot in Discord. It will respond with a pairing code.
Send to your agent on an existing channel:

“Approve this Discord pairing code: <CODE>”



Or via CLI:
openclaw pairing list discord
openclaw pairing approve discord <CODE>
Optional: Enable Guild Workspace
Once DMs work, you can enable full server access:
Add your server to the guild allowlist:
Ask your agent: “Add my Discord Server ID <server_id> to the guild allowlist”
Allow responses without @mention:
Ask your agent: “Allow my agent to respond on this server without having to be @mentioned”
Or via config:
{
  channels: {
    discord: {
      groupPolicy: "allowlist",
      guilds: {
        "YOUR_SERVER_ID": {
          requireMention: false
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
Telegram
Setup Steps
Step 1: Create a bot via BotFather

Open Telegram and search for @BotFather (verify the handle is exactly @BotFather)
Send /newbot and follow the prompts (give it a name and username)
Copy the Bot Token — looks like 123456789:ABCdef...

Step 2: Configure the bot token
openclaw config set channels.telegram.enabled true --json
openclaw config set channels.telegram.botToken '"YOUR_BOT_TOKEN"' --json
openclaw gateway restart
Or via config file:
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      enabled: true,
      botToken: "YOUR_BOT_TOKEN",
      dmPolicy: "pairing",
      groups: { "*": { requireMention: true } }
    }
  }
}
Step 3: Pair your account

DM your bot in Telegram — it’ll respond with a pairing code
Send the code to your agent on an existing channel:

“Approve this Telegram pairing code: <CODE>”



Or via CLI:
openclaw pairing list telegram
openclaw pairing approve telegram <CODE>
Pairing codes expire after 1 hour.
Finding your Telegram User ID
Option 1 — From your bot’s logs:
openclaw logs --follow
Then DM your bot and look for from.id in the logs.
Option 2 — Via Bot API:
curl "api.telegram.org/bot<YOUR_BOT_TOKEN>/getUpdates"
Option 3 — Third-party bot (less private):
Forward any message to @userinfobot or @getidsbot — they’ll reply with your user ID.
Optional: Switch to Allowlist Mode (Recommended for Production)
Instead of pairing mode, you can lock down access by user ID:
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      enabled: true,
      botToken: "YOUR_BOT_TOKEN",
      dmPolicy: "allowlist",
      allowFrom: ["YOUR_USER_ID"]
    }
  }
}
Groups
Privacy mode note: Telegram bots default to Privacy Mode — they can only see messages directed at them. To let the bot read all group messages:

Go to @BotFather → /setprivacy → select Disable
Remove and re-add the bot to the group for the change to take effect

Allow any group member:
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      groups: {
        "-1001234567890": {
          groupPolicy: "open",
          requireMention: false
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
Allow specific users in a group:
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      groups: {
        "-1001234567890": {
          requireMention: true,
          allowFrom: ["USER_ID_1", "USER_ID_2"]
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
To get a group chat ID: forward any group message to @userinfobot or check openclaw logs --follow after sending a message in the group.

python.title: python
python.content:
## virtual environments


 check what version of Python: 


which python


 create your environment, type 


conda create -n <yourenvironmentname> python=3.7 anaconda (or miniconda)


 see a list of all of your conda environments


conda info -e


 activate your Conda environments


conda activate <yourenvironmentname>


 install a specific package, type 


conda install -n <yourenvironmentname> [package]


 deactivate the current active Conda environments


conda deactivate.


 delete a Conda environment and all of its related packages


conda remove -n <yourenvironmentname> --all


 list all conda modules


conda list

update python
1. conda update conda
2. conda install python=3.12
3. conda install anaconda-client
4. conda update anaconda
replace Anaconda with miniconda

Uninstall Anaconda:
- Open a terminal.
- Run the command conda install anaconda-clean.
- Then run anaconda-clean --yes to remove the configuration files.
- Manually remove the Anaconda directory, usually located at ~/anaconda3 or ~/opt/anaconda3
Ensure All Anaconda References Are Removed:

Check your system’s environment variables (especially PATH) to make sure there are no references to Anaconda.
For Windows, this can be done in System Properties under Environment Variables.
For macOS/Linux, check your .bashrc, .bash_profile, or .zshrc files.


Install Miniconda:

Download Miniconda from the official website: Miniconda Download Page.
Choose the version that matches your operating system and desired Python version.


Install Miniconda:

For macOS/Linux:

Open a terminal.
Navigate to the directory containing the downloaded file.
Run the command bash Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh (adjust the file name as needed).
Follow the on-screen prompts.




Verify Installation:

Open a new terminal or command prompt.
Run conda list. If Miniconda is installed correctly, this command should list the installed packages.


Reinstall Necessary Packages:

Use conda install package_name to reinstall any packages you need that were previously managed by Anaconda.



pip install
If you have a non-conda pip as your default pip but conda python is your default python (as below)
>which -a pip
/home/<user>/.local/bin/pip   
/home/<user>/.conda/envs/newenv/bin/pip
/usr/bin/pip
 
>which -a python
/home/<user>/.conda/envs/newenv/bin/python
/usr/bin/python
Then instead of just calling pip install <package>, you can use the module flag -m with python so that it uses the anaconda python for the installation
python -m pip install <package>
This installs the package to the anaconda library directory rather than to the library directory associated with (the non-anaconda) pip
EDIT: The reason this works is as follows: the command pip references a specific pip file/shortcut (which -a pip tells you which one). Similarly, the command python references a specific python file (which -a python tells you which one). For one reason or another these two commands can become unsynchronized, so that your ‘default’ pip is in a different folder than your default python, and therefore is associated with a different version of python.
In contrast, the python -m pip construction does not use the shortcut that the pip command points to. Instead, it asks python to find its version of pip and use that version to install a package.
run .py in VScode interactive notebook
# %%

make interactive plots

install PyQt: pip install PyQt5
use magic command: %matplotlib qt

generate pdf from ipynb using nbconvert
customize the latex template

 reference: link1 link2

home/opt/anaconda3/share/jupyter/nbconvert/templates/latex/base.tex.j2

((* block date *))

((*- if nb.metadata["latex_metadata"]: -*))

((*- if nb.metadata["latex_metadata"]["date"]: -*))

\date{((( nb.metadata["latex_metadata"]["date"] )))}

((*- endif *))

((*- else: *))

\date{}

((*- endif *))

((* endblock date *))


 open metadata of ipynb in vscode

command palette: edit cell tags (metadata at the bottom of json file)

display dataframes side by side
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
 
class display(object):
	"""Display HTML representation of multiple objects"""
	template = """<div style="float: left; padding: 10px;">
	<p style='font-family:"Courier New", Courier, monospace'>{0}</p>{1}
	</div>"""
 
	def __init__(self, *args):
		self.args = args
	
	def _repr_html_(self):
		return '\n'.join(self.template.format(a, eval(a)._repr_html_()) for a in self.args)
	
	def __repr__(self):
		return '\n\n'.join(a + '\n' + repr(eval(a)) for a in self.args)
display('df1', 'df2')
install custom modules in odu  hpc
module load container_env python3
crun -c -p ~/envs/PROJECT_NAME
crun -p ~/envs/PROJECT_NAME python -m pip install PACKAGE_NAME
progress bar
from tqdm import tqdm
import time
 
# Outer loop
for i in tqdm(range(10), desc='Outer Loop'):
    # Some processing in the outer loop
    time.sleep(0.1)
    
    # Middle loop
    for j in tqdm(range(5), desc='Middle Loop', leave=False):
        # Some processing in the middle loop
        time.sleep(0.1)
 
        # Inner loop
        for k in tqdm(range(3), desc='Inner Loop', leave=False):
            # Some processing in the inner loop
            time.sleep(0.1)
load dictionary from npz file
In the context of NumPy and .npz files, [()] is used to access the contents of a structured array as a regular Python dictionary.
 
When you load a .npz file containing a dictionary, NumPy stores the dictionary keys and values in a structured array format. Accessing a key from this structured array returns an array-like object representing the value associated with that key. However, to retrieve the actual value in a format that behaves like a regular Python dictionary, you need to use [()].
 
Here's how it works:
 
When you access a key from the structured array like data['my_dict'], you get a structured array object.
Adding [()] at the end of data['my_dict'] allows you to retrieve the contents of the structured array as a regular Python dictionary.
So, [()] is essentially a way to "unpack" the contents of the structured array and convert it into a more familiar Python dictionary format.
 
In summary, [()] is used to access the contents of a structured array and convert it into a regular Python dictionary.
